summary: Flask web application with JWT authentication, YAML parsing, external HTTP calls, and Jinja2 templating. The dependency combination presents significant attack surface: PyYAML enables deserialization attacks, PyJWT requires careful verification, requests enables SSRF, and Jinja2 enables SSTI if user input reaches templates.

surfaces.description: HTTP endpoints defined in Flask application accepting untrusted user input. app.py is the entry point; api.py likely defines API routes. All request parameters, headers, and body data are potential injection vectors.
PyJWT 2.8.0 used for authentication. Common vulnerabilities include algorithm confusion (none algorithm), missing signature verification, weak secrets, and improper claims validation.
PyYAML 6.0.1 can execute arbitrary code if yaml.load() is called without Loader=SafeLoader. Any endpoint accepting YAML input is a remote code execution vector.
requests 2.31.0 makes outbound HTTP calls. If URLs are constructed from user input, this enables SSRF attacks against internal services, cloud metadata endpoints, or other restricted resources.
Jinja2 3.1.2 templating engine. If user input is interpolated into templates via string formatting rather than template variables, Server-Side Template Injection (SSTI) enables remote code execution.

Data models that likely interact with a database or data store. Potential for SQL injection if raw queries are used, mass assignment if model fields are not properly guarded, or insecure direct object references.

Werkzeug 3.1.3 handles request parsing, file uploads, and debugging. The Werkzeug debugger, if enabled in production, exposes an interactive Python console. File upload handling may allow path traversal.
